description.html: Square brackets [ ] are used to create or access an Array, an ordered list of values stored under a single variable name. You can put any values inside (numbers, strings, even other arrays), separated by commas: e.g. let colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]. To read a specific item back, use its index (position) inside brackets, counting from 0: colors[0] gives you "red".
description.text: Square brackets [ ] are used to create or access an Array, an ordered list of values stored under a single variable name. You can put any values inside (numbers, strings, even other arrays), separated by commas: e.g. let colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]. To read a specific item back, use its index (position) inside brackets, counting from 0: colors[0] gives you "red".

description.html: Curly braces { } have two main jobs in JavaScript. First, they create an Object — a collection of related values stored as key: value pairs: let person = { name: "Alice", age: 25 }. Access a value with dot notation: person.name gives you "Alice". Second, they wrap the body of functions, loops, and if-statements, everything between the braces is the block of code that runs together.
description.text: Curly braces { } have two main jobs in JavaScript. First, they create an Object — a collection of related values stored as key: value pairs: let person = { name: "Alice", age: 25 }. Access a value with dot notation: person.name gives you "Alice". Second, they wrap the body of functions, loops, and if-statements, everything between the braces is the block of code that runs together.

description.html: Parentheses ( ) serve a few roles in JavaScript. After a function name they hold the arguments (inputs) you pass into it: console.log("hello"). Inside conditions like if ( ) or loops like while ( ) they wrap the expression being tested. They also control the order of operations in expressions, just like in math: (2 + 3) * 4 adds first, then multiplies.
